upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very shallow, nearly isothermal boundary layer with a surface temp of 13°C rapidly capping thermals near the 850mb level (~1491m / ~4900 ft MSL). The sfc-to-850mb lapse rate is an extremely weak 1.88°C/km — far below dry adiabatic (9.8) and even moist adiabatic (~6.5), indicating strong convective inhibition and a well-capped, stable air mass. Upper winds veer from S at 850mb (210°/14 kts) through SSW at 500mb (215°/30 kts), with no meaningful wind shear hazard at flying altitudes, but thermal development will be severely limited by the stable layer.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. Noteworthy METAR observations: KSFO reporting CB distant east at 2056Z, gusts to 22 kts from 270°, suggesting active convection inland and a strengthening W-NW sea breeze push into the evening. KRHV at 2150Z shows 310°/10 kts with temps reaching 26°C — classic valley heating with NW sea breeze established. KAPC (Napa) at 2154Z: 200°/12 kts, confirming the S-SW gradient aloft is holding. KHAF (Half Moon Bay) near calm at 6-8 kts SW — marine layer thin with FEW085 cloud base, SST 14.6-14.7°C (cool), consistent with shallow but persistent marine influence. No significant aviation hazards.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's 1750 ft launch is a thermally driven site needing sufficient BL development and light-to-moderate S/SW winds to unlock ridge and thermal soaring. Ideal conditions are a stable morning followed by surface heating that breaks the cap by late morning, with winds 8–15 kts S-SSW. Sea breeze arrival from the NW in the afternoon can produce punchy rotor below ridge level.
sites.todaySetup: The decisive factor today is the extremely weak lapse rate and strong convective cap, limiting thermal development significantly. BL heights at launch stay below 400m (~1300 ft AGL) through noon, only reaching ~1000m (~3300 ft) briefly around 1–2 PM with CAPE building to 290–490 J/kg — enough for some thermal punch but under a hard cap. Surface winds are light-moderate S/SW in the morning (6–8 kts) shifting more westerly by afternoon (9–10 kts, 270–295°). The sea breeze front pushes through around 3–5 PM with winds clocking to 295–310° at 9–11 kts, which compromises launch conditions for this S-facing site. KRHV confirms inland heating (26°C, 310°/10 kts by 2150Z), supporting the sea breeze arrival. Cloud cover is variable — heavy AM overcast clearing midday, then re-clouding by 2–3 PM which will suppress any brief thermal window. The short thermal window between 1–3 PM with elevated CAPE but heavy cloud cover, tricky wind direction shift, and a punchy/capped BL makes this a marginal day at best.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 6–7 kts S/SSW, BL only 90–375m — well below launch height. Heavy overcast suppresses heating entirely. No usable lift; launch is not viable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Brief partial clearing helps BL climb to 455–1015m with CAPE spiking to 290–450 J/kg by 1–2 PM. Winds 7–9 kts SSW-SW could allow a thermal flight, but cloud cover returns to 100% by 2 PM and the cap limits ceiling. A narrow window may exist 12:30–2 PM for experienced pilots, but conditions are punchy under capped convection.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind shifts to W/NW (272–296°) by 3 PM at 9–10 kts — unfavorable for this S-facing launch and rotor risk increases below ridge level. BL collapses to 635–445m by 4–5 PM. Not recommended.

verdict.hazards: Strong cap limits thermal ceiling; CAPE spikes to 450–490 J/kg under cloud deck suggest punchy thermals; W/NW wind shift by 3 PM creates rotor risk; heavy morning overcast

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower launch, S-facing training hill
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch at Ed Levin is a lower training hill suitable for P2 pilots in light, steady S/SSW winds of 6–12 kts. It relies on surface thermal triggering and valley breeze. It is more sheltered than the upper launch but equally susceptible to afternoon sea breeze wind shifts.
sites.todaySetup: Like the upper launch, the lower hill is compromised today by a stable, capped atmosphere with a very weak lapse rate. BL heights are only 80–185m at 8–9 AM — below the lower launch height itself — meaning no thermal support in the morning. The midday window is slightly more accessible here given the lower launch elevation: BL reaches 455–1220m between noon and 2 PM with light CAPE. However, the same overcast-then-cap pattern applies, and the afternoon W/NW wind shift (to 285–314° by 3–5 PM) is directly cross-to-unfavorable for this S-facing hill. Winds stay light overall (5–11 kts), which could allow some soaring near the hill in the 12:30–2 PM window, but expect punchy, capped thermals. This is a marginal training day at best.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL under 355m, 100% overcast, winds 3–6 kts SW — too light and stable for even the lower launch. Ground school or wait conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Partial clearing allows BL to climb to 455–1220m. Winds 6–8 kts SSW could provide gentle ridge lift and weak thermals. Best window for a conservative P2 flight near the hill, but expect variable conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind shifts to W/NW (285–314°) at 8–11 kts by 3–5 PM — cross to unfavorable for this S-facing hill. Sea breeze arrival ends the thermal window. Land before 2:30 PM.

verdict.hazards: Afternoon W/NW shift makes launch cross/unfavorable; capped thermals; morning overcast delays any heating

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site that needs consistent W/NW onshore wind of 10–20 kts to work. The cliffs generate dynamic lift when wind is perpendicular (WSW–WNW). Marine layer and fog are the primary hazards — if visibility is compromised or the wind goes too light or too strong, it doesn't fly. KHAF METAR and buoy data are key for assessing marine layer depth.
sites.todaySetup: This is the strongest candidate for flying today among the coastal sites. Mussel Rock is currently showing functional conditions: KSFO reporting 250–270° at 10–14G22 kts and KHAF at 7 kts SW with FEW085 ceiling and 10SM visibility, indicating the marine layer has a high enough base and the site is in the clear. GFS/HRRR both agree on 5–9 kts WSW/W surface winds through early afternoon, building to 8–9 kts by 1–3 PM with consistent W/WSW direction (235–252°) — right on the money for this WNW-facing ridge. SST at 14.6–14.7°C (cool) limits deep marine layer intrusion but does sustain a coastal stable layer. The buoy at Half Moon Bay shows only 0.8m/10s wave height — mild swell, no significant wave energy concern. Winds are on the lighter side of ideal through midday (~6–9 kts surface) but should provide soarable ridge lift. Afternoon looks best with 9 kts and cleaner air. Low precipitation risk all day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 5–6 kts WSW — lighter than ideal but potentially soarable on the ridge face. 100% cloud cover but ceiling appears adequate (KHAF FEW085). Marginal ridge lift; expect low, scratchy soaring.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind builds to 6–9 kts W/WSW (235–252°) — best window of the day. 85% cloud cover partially clearing by 2 PM (32% at 2 PM per HRRR). Solid ridge lift, favorable direction, and improving visibility.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds hold 7–9 kts WSW through 5–6 PM, remaining favorable for ridge soaring. Cloud cover becomes variable (7–100%). Sustained flyable window through late afternoon.

verdict.hazards: Lighter wind early may limit altitude; variable cloud/marine layer; cool SST (14.6°C) could deepen marine layer if onshore flow strengthens

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a combined thermal and ridge site, needing S/SW winds of 8–15 kts and sufficient BL development for good flights. Like Ed Levin, it depends on surface heating breaking the cap by late morning, with the sea breeze front arriving from the NW in the afternoon. Wind direction from SSW is ideal.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same fundamental problem as Ed Levin today: the KOAK sounding reveals a very weak 1.88°C/km lapse rate with a strong cap near 850mb, severely limiting BL development and thermal quality. BL heights start at only 90m at 8 AM, climb slowly to 375–455m by 11 AM–noon, then briefly reach 840–1015m between 1–2 PM with CAPE peaking at 290–490 J/kg — thermally active but hard-capped. Wind direction is favorable (SSW 198–243°) through the midday window before shifting to W/WNW (272–295°) by 3 PM. KRHV's 310°/10 kts and 26°C by 2150Z confirms the sea breeze arrives by mid-afternoon, ending the thermal window. The brief 1–2 PM window with CAPE ~300–500 J/kg could yield punchy, short-lived thermals, but the heavy cloud cover returning by 2–3 PM (100%) and the imminent wind shift make this a challenging day. Experienced P3 pilots may find a narrow soarable window.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 100% overcast, BL only 90–375m — entirely below ridge height. Winds 6–7 kts SSW, stable. No viable lift; do not launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Partial clearing (14% cloud cover at noon) allows BL to climb toward 1000m by 1–2 PM with CAPE spiking to 290–450 J/kg. Winds 7–9 kts SSW in the sweet spot. Short but potentially rewarding window for P3 pilots comfortable with punchy, capped thermals.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind clocks to W-WNW (272–295°) by 3 PM at 9–10 kts — unfavorable for this S/SW-facing site. BL collapses, cloud cover returns to 100%, and sea breeze rotor risk increases. Land by 2:30 PM.

verdict.hazards: Strong stable cap limits thermal ceiling; CAPE spikes suggest punchy thermals; W/NW wind shift by 3 PM; heavy AM overcast delays heating window

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Friday Apr 10 looks windier and more overcast. GFS/HRRR both show SSW winds building to 15–16 kts by 2–4 PM with 80m wind levels reaching 18–20 kts — approaching the upper limit for this site. BL develops well (1095–1285m) suggesting better thermal potential, but 40–43% precip probability and 77–100% cloud cover much of the day will suppress heating and introduce moisture. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 14–17 kts. Marginal to no-fly; winds may be too strong and gusty by afternoon.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar story to the upper launch — SSW winds 11–16 kts with 80m winds 13–19 kts, better BL development (645–1285m), but precip probability at 22–40% and near-total cloud cover. Tomorrow.io shows gusts 9–15 kts from S/SSW. The lower launch may see a narrow flyable window midday if clouds break, but increasing wind speeds and moisture make this marginal. Watch precip probability trends.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Friday could be a decent day at Mussel Rock. GFS/HRRR show SSW winds 7–12 kts (221–217°) building through the day — favorable direction for this W-facing ridge. Tomorrow.io shows 7–10 kts with gusts to 12–14 kts and poor visibility (0.2 km in the morning) suggesting significant marine layer/fog early. If the marine layer lifts by midday, afternoon (2–4 PM, 10–12 kts SSW) looks flyable. Key uncertainty: fog burn-off timing. Watch KHAF METARs in the morning.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Friday looks similar to Ed Levin — SSW winds building to 15–16 kts by afternoon with 80m winds 18–20 kts. BL develops to 1095–1110m suggesting good thermal potential if skies clear, but 41–43% precip probability and persistent cloud cover are significant concerns. Tomorrow.io shows gusts 10–16 kts SSW. Could be a punchy, strong-thermal day if precip holds off, but P3+ only given wind speeds and instability.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Saturday Apr 11 shows increasing wind and high precipitation risk (49–95%). GFS shows SSW winds 10–18 kts with 80m winds reaching 24–27 kts and BL heights to 1865–2030m — very active thermally but likely wet. With 76–95% precip probability by afternoon and 100% cloud cover all day, this looks like a rain day. NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Saturday conditions mirror the upper launch with precip probability 44–94% and strong SSW winds 10–18 kts. High BL (1485–2030m) signals significant atmospheric instability. Rain likely by afternoon. NO FLY.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Saturday Apr 11 is concerning at Mussel Rock. GFS shows winds building sharply to 18–21 kts SSW by afternoon with 80m winds 20–28 kts — well above comfortable limits for this ridge site. Precip probability reaches 62–94%. Very likely a stormy, unflyable day. NO FLY.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Saturday is shaping up as the worst day of the period — SSW winds 10–18 kts with 80m winds 11–27 kts, BL to 2015m, and precip probability 47–95% all day. 100% cloud cover. High CAPE (100–280 J/kg) under a moist unstable regime suggests potential for convective precipitation. NO FLY.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: No Day 3 (April 12) GFS data available in this dataset. Based on the trend of an approaching Pacific system through Apr 11, conditions are likely to remain unsettled. Monitor updated forecasts.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: No Day 3 data available. System passage pattern suggests possible clearing behind the front by Apr 12 afternoon, but winds may be strong post-frontal NW. Check updated models.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: No Day 3 data available. Post-frontal NW flow could actually benefit Mussel Rock on Apr 12 if winds moderate to 12–18 kts. Worth monitoring.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: No Day 3 data available. Post-frontal clearing possible Apr 12, but strong NW winds may persist. Check updated GFS/HRRR for post-system conditions.

watchlist: Mussel Rock AM fog: Tomorrow.io shows 0.2 km visibility Friday morning — monitor KHAF METARs for burn-off timing before heading out
Approaching Pacific system: precip probability spikes to 44–95% Saturday Apr 11 at all sites — flying window may close Friday afternoon
Today's sea breeze front: KRHV already showing 310°/10 kts NW push by 2150Z — inland sites (Ed Levin, Mission Peak) will have wind shifts to W/NW by 2:30–3 PM, ending the thermal window early
CAPE under cap today: 290–490 J/kg with strong convective inhibition means any thermals that do break will be punchy and irregular — not a smooth thermal day
tldr: Mussel Rock is today's best bet — WSW ridge wind of 8–9 kts and adequate ceiling make for a solid 11 AM–4 PM window. Inland sites (Ed Levin, Mission Peak) have a narrow 12:30–2:30 PM thermal window but it's capped, punchy, and short before the NW sea breeze shuts it down — P3+ only. A Pacific system arrives Saturday bringing rain to all sites, so Friday afternoon may be the last flyable window of the stretch.
